sourceMovie.title: Le Trou
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Le Trou (1960) as a drama theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The drama and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Layer Cake
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Le Trou clicked for you, Layer Cake (2004) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and thriller, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Cul-de-sac
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Le Trou clicked for you, Cul-de-sac (1966) is a strong next stop: both trade in drama and thriller, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Le Trou?
faq.answer: Try Layer Cake, Cul-de-sac, The Machinist—each shares DNA with Le Trou while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Le Trou?
faq.answer: Le Trou (1960) is best described as Drama, Thriller, Crime. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Le Trou worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 8.3/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this drama lane.
